pi_items.source: POE5 (Principles of Econometrics 5th Edition) dataset repository at principlesofeconometrics.com; confirmed in Ecdat R package documentation and multiple econometrics textbooks
POE5 data definition file (principlesofeconometrics.com/poe5/data/def/mroz.def); confirmed by multiple R package documentation (Ecdat, sampleSelection, causaldata packages)

POE5 data definition; Mroz dataset contains 428 women with positive work hours and 325 women with zero hours worked in 1975. Source: Mroz (1987) study based on 1975 Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID)
POE5 dataset description and multiple econometrics sources (Mroz 1987 article, Ecdat R package, textbook references)
POE5 data definition file; Mroz (1987) 'The Sensitivity of an Empirical Model of Married Women's Hours of Work to Economic and Statistical Assumptions,' Econometrica 55(4): 657-678

Verified from gretl heckit.inp script demonstrating Wooldridge's specification where nwifeinc appears in selection equation but not in outcome equation, making it a valid exclusion restriction. Cross-referenced with LOST (Learning Statistics Online) documentation on Heckman correction models

S-S R tutorial (s-saisw.github.io/rtutorial/heckit.html) - automatic Heckit estimation on Mroz dataset shows education coefficient of 0.1187 (SE: 0.0341) in outcome equation. Alternative implementations may produce slightly different values depending on estimation method (MLE vs two-step)
Specified in task.json metadata; verified value of 0.1187 falls within tolerance range [0.089, 0.129] suggesting tolerance is appropriately set

privileged_info_summary: The Mroz dataset (mroz.gdt) is real, cross-sectional labor force data from the 1975 Panel Study of Income Dynamics containing exactly 753 observations: 428 women with positive wages and 325 censored observations (non-workers). The dataset is sourced from the POE5 (Principles of Econometrics 5th Edition) repository and is widely used for demonstrating Heckman selection models. The exclusion variable nwifeinc (non-wife income) is correctly specified as appearing in the selection equation for labor force participation (lfp) but not in the outcome wage equation, making it a valid identifying restriction. The education coefficient in the outcome equation from canonical Heckman-MLE estimation is approximately 0.1187, which falls within the stated tolerance of ±0.02 from the claimed value of 0.109.
